narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimates a 70.8% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first try, based on patterns from thousands of past students. You have a solid LSAT score of 40, which is above average, an undergraduate GPA of 3.10 that's just a bit below typical, and come from an upper-middle-class family background. You're a white female planning to attend full-time, which aligns with the majority of students in our data. However, your school decile rankings stand out as particularly low—both your Year 1 and Year 3 deciles are 2, well below the averages of about 5.7 and 5.5—placing you in the lower performance groups compared to peers. These academic deciles were the most influential factors in the model's decision, each pushing the prediction toward expecting a bar failure rather than a pass. Next in influence came your LSAT score, which surprisingly nudged the odds slightly toward failure in this context, followed by your family income bracket. Your undergraduate GPA, racial background, gender, and full-time status had progressively smaller impacts, all leaning the prediction toward a fail but not as strongly. In essence, the model weighed your early and later school performance as the top concerns, seeing them as stronger signals of bar struggles than your other strengths. Compared to typical students who pass, you're underrepresented in those higher decile groups that correlate with success. The good news is you can take action: focus on boosting your academic deciles through better study habits or targeted tutoring before reapplying, as those were the biggest levers. Retaking the LSAT to push even higher or strengthening your GPA narrative with recent achievements could also help shift future predictions. Many students in similar spots have improved by addressing these gaps—keep pursuing your goal, and consider bar prep resources early to build confidence. We're rooting for your next steps.
